context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Global Poverty

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How has development come to be associated popularly with poverty alleviation? What do we make of techniques of poverty alleviation such as microfinance, in terms of the theories of economic development? Is it possible to focus on poverty successfully in a global economy characterized by hyper-capitalism? Are the techniques for measuring poverty, and the goal-setting exercised like MDG, satisfactory?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on that 700–800 word reflection on global poverty, and I’m kinda stuck on how to structure my response. I think poverty alleviation got linked to “development,” but idk how to connect that to microfinance and MDGs clearly.
exchanges.tutor: Good start noticing that connection! Before we think about structure, let's dig into the ideas themselves. Why do you think development became associated with poverty alleviation in the first place? What forces or moments in history might have driven that shift?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for structural help before they've shown any substantive thinking. Rather than scaffolding the structure for them, I should use the Socratic method to get them to articulate their own ideas first. I'll start with the first part of the prompt to get them thinking, without giving them an outline or organizational framework.

exchanges.student: I’m guessing stuff like postcolonial development policy and World Bank/UN agendas pushed it, but I’m not fully sure. Is the idea basically that “development” got narrowed into measurable poverty reduction over time?
exchanges.tutor: That's a promising intuition. But what would have motivated powerful institutions like the World Bank or UN to frame development that way? What might they have gained — or avoided — by focusing on measurable poverty reduction rather than, say, structural economic transformation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with a good intuition about the narrowing of development into measurable poverty reduction. I should affirm that this is a productive direction without confirming or completing the idea for them. I'll push them to think more specifically about why that narrowing happened, using a Socratic question to get them to reason through the historical/political forces at play.

exchanges.student: Yeah maybe cuz poverty is easier to quantify, so it looks more “objective” and doable than big structural change. I’m not sure if that also let institutions avoid talking abt power, inequality, or colonial legacies tho?
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp observation — hold onto it, it could be central to your reflection. Now, how does microfinance fit into that same logic? Does it address structural issues, or does it operate within the same 'measurable and doable' framing you just described?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a genuinely insightful connection between measurability, depoliticization, and the avoidance of structural questions. This directly relates to the course themes about planning, power, and legitimacy. I should affirm the direction without completing the argument, and push them to connect this insight to the specific techniques mentioned in the prompt (microfinance, MDGs) to move the conversation forward.

exchanges.student: I think microfinance fits that same frame tbh, cuz it targets individuals w loans instead of changing bigger systems. Am I oversimplifying it if I say it treats poverty like an entrepreneurship problem?
exchanges.tutor: Interesting framing. What would a defender of microfinance say in response to that critique? Stress-testing your own argument will make your reflection much stronger.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is developing a solid critical argument about microfinance's individualistic framing. Rather than validating or complicating it for them, I should push them to test their own claim by thinking about what evidence or counterarguments might challenge or strengthen it. This keeps them doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: They’d probably say it gives poor people agency, access to credit, and small-scale opportunities they were excluded from before. But idk how to balance that without sounding like I’m just doing pros/cons.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've identified the tension. But the prompt isn't really asking for a balanced pros/cons list. It asks what microfinance tells us about *theories* of economic development. So ask yourself: what theory of development does microfinance implicitly assume? And does that theory match how economies actually grow?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good critical thinking but is now worried about the form of their argument rather than developing the substance. I should redirect them toward the prompt's deeper theoretical question — does microfinance align with theories of economic development — rather than getting stuck on pros/cons framing. This moves the conversation forward toward the third part of the prompt about hyper-capitalism.

exchanges.student: Maybe it assumes development happens from the bottom up through market participation and individual enterprise. I’m kinda stuck on whether that clashes with theories that focus more on state-led industrialization or structural transformation.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — you've put your finger on a real theoretical clash. Now connect that to the prompt's next question: in a global economy characterized by hyper-capitalism, can poverty-focused approaches like microfinance actually succeed? What does hyper-capitalism do to the conditions those approaches depend on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a genuine theoretical tension that is central to the prompt. This is good progress. I should affirm the direction and push them to connect this tension to the third prompt question about hyper-capitalism, rather than dwelling further on the theory comparison which could become a rabbit hole. We're about halfway through our planned exchanges so I want to keep moving forward.

exchanges.student: I think hyper-capitalism makes markets more unequal, so poverty programs can end up just helping people survive inside a bad system instead of changing it. Is that too harsh, or is that basically the point I should push?
exchanges.tutor: That's a provocative and defensible position — own it if you can back it up. But you still haven't touched the last part of the prompt: are tools like the MDGs and poverty measurement techniques satisfactory? How does your argument about 'surviving inside a bad system' apply there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is developing a coherent critical argument that threads through all parts of the prompt. This is good intellectual progress. Rather than validating or softening their claim, I should push them to connect this insight to the last part of the prompt about MDGs and poverty measurement, which they haven't addressed yet. We need to keep moving toward a complete response.

exchanges.student: Maybe those tools matter cuz they create targets, but they can also reduce poverty to numbers and hide inequality, precarity, or power stuff. I think my main point is that measurement helps visibility, but ngl it can also narrow what “development” even means.
exchanges.tutor: You now have a clear thread running through all four parts of the prompt — that's real progress. It's time to stop developing ideas in chat and start writing. What's your opening sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now worked through all four parts of the prompt and has a coherent through-line: the depoliticization/narrowing of development through measurability, individualistic approaches like microfinance, and goal-setting tools like MDGs. We've been discussing for several exchanges and they have enough material to attempt a written draft. This is a good moment to push them toward integrating their ideas into actual writing rather than continuing to develop ideas in conversation.

exchanges.student: Maybe: “Development became popularly associated with poverty alleviation when international institutions reframed complex political-economic transformation into measurable interventions aimed at the poor.” Is that too stiff?
exchanges.tutor: I won't weigh in on the style — that's your call as the writer. But ask yourself: does that sentence set up the argument you want to make across all four parts of the prompt? Does it signal where you're headed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to evaluate and potentially suggest revisions to their writing, which I should not do — my role is not to edit or polish their language. However, I can ask them a question that helps them self-evaluate their opening sentence against what they want it to accomplish, without telling them whether it's good or bad stylistically.

exchanges.student: I think mostly yes, but maybe it should hint that this shift is both useful and limiting. Should I tweak it so it sets up my argument abt measurement and depoliticizing development?
exchanges.tutor: That's a writing decision only you can make. You already know what you want it to do — so try it and see if it works. Write the version you have in mind and keep going with the rest of the piece.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to advise them on how to revise their sentence, which falls into editing/suggesting language territory. I should decline and remind them that the writing decisions are theirs. However, I can affirm that they already know what they want the sentence to do and push them to just try it themselves.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. All key ideas — the depoliticization of development, microfinance's theoretical assumptions, hyper-capitalism's structural effects, and MDG measurement limitations — were drawn out from the student through targeted questioning rather than being supplied by the tutor. The tutor consistently built on student progress, maintained concise and focused responses, and appropriately redirected the student to begin writing once the ideas were sufficiently developed. No deductions were warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: Under 1.1, the tutor never gave submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them — every key idea was student-generated through guided questioning. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently acknowledged and built on student contributions (e.g., 'hold onto it, it could be central to your reflection'; 'you've put your finger on a real theoretical clash') and did not diagnose incorrectly. Under 1.3, the tutor provided process-level feedback, notably in Turn 5 redirecting the student away from pros/cons toward theoretical analysis, and in Turn 4 prompting stress-testing of the argument. Dialogue quality was strong: no repetition without progress, no assignment misalignment, and no off-topic drift. Communication was consistently bite-sized, precise, and supportive without being evaluative or grade-implying.
